title: The Assistant You Own
description: Standalone companion to the essay of the same name — where should your 24/7 AI assistant live, and who owns the place? The most important part of your AI assistant is not the AI. From the 'Beyond the Sea of App Icons' series.

beats.text: The assistant you own. Every company now promises an AI assistant that is with you twenty four hours a day. This is a standalone essay from my series on AI-native computing — about the one question nobody puts on the slide.

eyebrow.label: A standalone essay · Beyond the Sea of App Icons

slide.title: The Assistant **You Own**
slide.subtitle: Where should your AI assistant live — and {primary:who owns the place?}
slide.author: Satoshi Nakajima
chips: 🏠 your PC as your personal cloud
🔄 the model is the swappable part

beats.text: Seven in the morning. My assistant has been working all night. The podcast feeds came in at three. A bill reminder is waiting. The flashcards are updated. And from the train, I ask it about my restaurant list. Not some assistant — mine. The one that has been accumulating my life for a year.

image.html:
7:04 AM · on the train
It worked all night.
Your assistant — at home, always on. Your phone is just a window into it.
7:04
📶 🔋
🎙
3 new episodes ingested
3:12 AM · while you slept
🔔
Rent recurs on the 10th
reminder raised · 6:00 AM
🎴
Flashcards updated
12 new cards ready
What was that ramen place on the Chuo line?
Ramen Kaze — Nakano. You rated it ★★★★ in March.

beats.text: The promise is real. But here is the housing question. Where does this assistant live — and who owns the place where it lives?

eyebrow.label: The question nobody puts on the slide

slide.quote: Where does your assistant {primary:live} — and {warning:who owns the place} where it lives?
slide.author: The housing question

beats.text: For years, I argued in public that the assistant should live in the cloud, not in a device. I was half right. Any device, always on — that part was correct. My mistake: I said 'the cloud' like people say 'the grid' — as if it were neutral. It isn't. I never asked who owns it.

left.title: What I got right

items.text: Always on — works while you sleep

left.footer: These are what make an assistant an assistant

content.value: “The cloud” is not neutral infrastructure. It is **a specific company's computers.**

content.text: If your assistant's self lives on their computers — they own your assistant.

beats.text: We know what renting software feels like. The redesign you didn't ask for. The pivot. The price change. The beloved service, discontinued. Robin Sloan named what renting can never give you: sovereignty — it won't change unless you want it to. Now raise the stakes. A discontinued assistant doesn't lose you photos. It loses the accumulated self of the thing that organizes your life.

slide.title: You already know this feeling
slide.footer: Sloan's word for what renting never gives you: sovereignty — “it won't change unless we want it to.”

beats.text: And here is the harder half. It's easy to nod when the warning names Meta or Google. But an assistant whose self lives with Anthropic or Open A I is rented all the same. I build on Anthropic's models every single day, and I expect to for years. The model vendor deserves my A P I calls. It should not own my assistant.

eyebrow.label: The harder half — model providers too

slide.quote: The model vendor deserves my API calls. It should {danger:not own my assistant.}
slide.author: — said while building on Anthropic's models, daily
slide.role: Meta · Google · Anthropic · OpenAI — capture is capture

beats.text: Strip an assistant down to its parts. The model is brilliant — and a commodity in the making. Several excellent ones, leapfrogging each other every few months. You should want to swap them freely, like engines. The accumulation is the opposite. Your data. Its memory. The apps built for exactly you. Worthless to everyone else — and irreplaceable to you.

slide.title: The two parts of an assistant

left.title: The model

items: Brilliant — and a commodity in the making

left.footer: The part they own — and market

items: Your data, its memory of you
The apps grown for exactly you

callout.text: A year of accumulation cannot be re-bought at any price. It can only be {warning:re-lived.}
callout.label: The stakes

beats.text: The most important part of your AI assistant is not the AI.

slide.quote: The most important part of your AI assistant is {primary:not the AI.}
slide.author: The Assistant You Own
slide.role: It's the accumulation — and the model should be the interchangeable part

beats.text: So here is the ownership test. Two questions. Could you switch models tomorrow — and keep your assistant? Could you move your assistant — and keep its memory? If either answer is no, you don't own an assistant. You have a subscription to one.

slide.title: Two questions

items.title: 1 · Switch models tomorrow — keep your assistant?
items.description: Every memory, every app, a new engine.

items.title: 2 · Move your assistant — keep its memory?
items.description: The whole self, on a disk you can carry.

slide.footer: If either answer is no — you don't own an assistant. You have a subscription to one.

beats.text: So the device is wrong, and the provider's cloud is wrong. What's left sounds almost quaint: your own computer. Always on, at home. Its whole self is files on a disk you own. Your phone becomes a thin window — and it never touches the PC directly. Both sides talk through a relay, and the house only dials out. No open port. Nothing to scan. Your own PC becomes your personal cloud.

image.html:
The third place
🏠
🖥️
the agent · always on
its whole self: files on your disk
memory · records · the apps it built
the house only dials out ⟶
relay · per-user queue
no open port · nothing to scan
📱
your phone
a thin window
💻
your laptop
another window
Your own PC becomes your personal cloud.

beats.text: The honest costs. Your requests still travel through a relay — the self stays home, but the errands travel. And today, self-hosting is for tinkerers: someone has to keep the disk backed up. But notice the difference. That is an engineering problem. Capture is a business model. Only one of these can be fixed.

slide.title: What I will not wave away

items.title: The errands travel
items.description: Requests transit a relay on someone's cloud. The self stays home; the errands don't.

items.title: Today: tinkerers only
items.description: Updates, backups, uptime — someone has to care. Closing this gap is real product work.

items.title: But note the difference
items.description: These are engineering problems. Capture is a business model — it can't be patched away.

beats.text: One more piece comes home, eventually. In the nineteen-sixties, computers were so expensive that everyone time-shared a distant mainframe. That era ended when the computer became personal. Today's models are in their time-sharing era. No hurry — when the day comes, nothing changes but an endpoint, pointing home instead of out. And the sovereignty is complete.

slide.title: The computer came home. The model will too.

items.title: Time-shared mainframes
items.description: Compute too expensive to own — everyone rents a slice

items.title: The computer becomes personal
items.description: The era ends in the most literal way possible

items.title: Models are time-shared
items.description: The engine is rented — contentedly, for now

items.title: The model comes home
items.description: Nothing changes but an endpoint — sovereignty complete

beats.text: The full essay goes deeper, and the six-part series behind it explains the architecture that makes an assistant ownable at all. The short version is this. The model is the engine — and engines are for swapping. Make sure the part that accumulates, the part that becomes a second self over the years, accumulates on a disk that belongs to you.

slide.title: The Assistant **You Own**
slide.subtitle: The architecture behind it: {primary:Beyond the Sea of App Icons}, Parts 1–6
